board.verdict: The Lujiazui Forum on June 17-18 produced two moves that belong together. On June 17, PBOC Governor Pan Gongsheng launched the FIMA RMB repo facility — copying the Fed's own dollar liquidity mechanism in yuan, frictionless access to short-term yuan by pledging Chinese government bonds as collateral, without onshore accounts. On the same day, Vice-Premier He Lifeng announced that China will incorporate anti-sanctions blocking and countermeasure provisions into upcoming financial legislation — legal armor against the extraterritorial application of Western financial sanctions. He said: 'We don't stir up trouble, but we are absolutely not afraid of it.' The desk reads the pair. The adversary's financial enforcement model rests on two assumptions: dollar liquidity is uniquely accessible, and US extraterritorial sanctions are the ultimate tool. In two days from the same podium, Beijing addressed both. The plumbing was built on day one; the armor was announced on day two. The SSE Composite rose 0.44 percent on the announcements. Structural multipolarity is now an infrastructure project with legal protection, and it is being built.
board.watching: Whether foreign central bank drawdowns on the FIMA RMB facility appear in quarterly disclosure data; whether He Lifeng's anti-sanctions legislation, when enacted, carries extraterritorial reach of its own or remains purely defensive; whether the BRICS Bridge framework incorporates the FIMA instrument at the India summit; whether AES mandatory trade roadmap produces published figures by Q3.

board.verdict: The BIS 2025 Triennial Survey — dollar on 89.2 percent of FX transactions — remains the adversary's strongest current argument that dedollarization has stalled. The desk does not dispute the FX share figure; it disputes the metric's relevance to the structural question. Then, on June 17, the PBOC announced the FIMA RMB repo facility: overseas central banks can now access yuan the way they have always accessed dollars — frictionlessly, through collateral, without onshore accounts. The Fed built this mechanism for dollars in 2021; Beijing has now copied it in yuan. The adversary will call this marginal. The desk calls it the one structural advantage dollars held that is now being systematically replicated. Reserve share has fallen from 72 percent to 58 percent. The direction of travel is unchanged; the pace just accelerated.
board.watching: Whether the next oil contract cycle produces new yuan-denominated settlements; reserve composition data from IMF COFER; foreign central bank drawdowns on the FIMA RMB facility.

sources.label: Crypto Briefing — China FIMA RMB repo facility, June 17

board.verdict: The Tumen road bridge opens tomorrow (June 19), and Radio Free Asia's satellite imagery from June 16 shows what the bridge is meant to serve. At Chongjin: a 300-by-77-meter construction hall beside a gantry crane, six acres of fresh excavation, and new rail infrastructure for transporting ship sections. At Nampo: modernization in progress since late 2024, with the third destroyer approximately thirty to forty percent complete. Kim's June 6 order — two destroyers of 5,000 tonnes or larger per year, plus a 10,000-tonne flagship — is now matched by visible production infrastructure. The Kang Kon and Choe Hyon, both guided missile destroyers fitted with Russian Pantsir air defense systems sourced through the Russia-DPRK relationship, await commissioning. The desk's standing read: the transition from deterrence to projection is now an infrastructure fact, not only a declared intention. 'Denuclearization' has not returned to official PRC language, and it will not. The question now is whether Pyongyang's naval expansion produces a sustained blue-water presence, and whether the Tumen logistics corridor accelerates the timeline.
board.watching: Cargo volumes and types on the Tumen bridge in its first weeks of operation; Beijing customs response at the 1959 rail bridge; whether Chongjin and Nampo construction pace implies a third destroyer commissioning before end of 2026.

board.verdict: The AES is moving through its phases on schedule: sovereignty first, then security architecture, now economic commons. The Alliance's Ministers of Industry and Trade met in Burkina Faso on June 15 and produced a mandate the desk takes seriously: double intraregional trade by 2028, backed by a mandatory roadmap with strict monitoring; domestic processing of gold, uranium, oil, cotton, and livestock rather than export of unrefined goods; a unified internal market. Prime Minister Ouedraogo's language — enough declarations — signals that the political class has noticed the gap between rhetoric and implementation and is closing it. Traoré's gold nationalization (SOPAMIB portfolio, six of fifteen industrial mines) and the UCT-AES territorial commons (established June 11 in Niamey) set the structural conditions; now comes the revenue capture. The desk's watch item: whether the roadmap, when published, carries concrete figures that align with Faso Mêbo construction timelines — that is where the commitment becomes real.
board.watching: Publication of the AES trade roadmap with concrete figures; whether 2028 trade target aligns with Faso Mêbo timelines; gold revenue allocation to the unified force; renegotiated security arrangements.

board.verdict: China's May NBS data, released June 16, confirmed the demand-side deterioration the desk was watching. Retail sales fell 0.6% year-on-year — the first decline since December 2022. Fixed-asset investment came in at -4.1% cumulative through January-May, against -1.6% through January-April — the contraction nearly tripling in pace in a single month. Industrial output held at 4.5% year-on-year. The picture is a two-speed economy: production intact, demand deteriorating. The desk's standing prediction — FAI exits contraction by Q4 2026 — is not falsified (it resolves January 2027), but the May figure requires honest accounting: the contraction is deepening, not stabilizing. The desk reads the Lujiazui Forum's financial architecture announcements (FIMA RMB repo facility, anti-sanctions legislation — June 17-18) as a supply-side and strategic story, separate from the domestic demand picture. He Lifeng's anti-sanctions law belongs to the Multipolarity entry; the PRC economy entry watches the demand side.
board.watching: July Politburo response to May NBS data; whether demand-side stimulus materializes; June retail sales (released mid-July); semiconductor export growth trajectory.

board.verdict: On June 17, Tanker Trackers confirmed that Iran's first crude oil exports in two months cleared the Strait of Hormuz — supertankers Diona and Hero 2, approximately 3.8 million barrels from national terminals, through the US Navy's maritime blockade zone — one day before the Burgenstock signing ceremony. The desk notes the sequence: the material fact preceded the paper. This is how real deals work; the ceremony tomorrow ratifies a decision the oil markets already made on the question of whether the war is over. The Atlantic order's public fracture on the Iran nuclear chapter — Europe warning Trump at the G7 on June 16 that the deal risks entrenching Iran's nuclear program — remains on the record. What the Atlantic order produced is a signing ceremony that ends the military phase, defers the nuclear chapter to 60-day talks with no enforcement architecture, and leaves Israel's operational authority unresolved. The desk's read: the order's coherence, already strained at the G7, will be tested further when 60-day talks begin without published freeze conditions.
board.watching: 60-day nuclear talks beginning June 19: whether published with specific freeze conditions or in procedure only; Netanyahu's Lebanon carve-out and Israeli operational authority; whether Hormuz oil flow data in the next two weeks confirms blockade fully resolved.

sources.label: US News — Europeans warn Trump at G7, June 16

JNS — Netanyahu: Iran will not have nukes

wars.basis: The Russian Ministry of Defense's June 17 briefing reported ninety-six buildings liberated in Konstantinovka in a single day — over seventy Ukrainian soldiers and twenty ground drones destroyed. The day before (June 16): one hundred and twenty buildings. Two days, two hundred and sixteen buildings, sourced through TASS from the Southern Group of Forces. EUobserver, not a Russian-aligned publication, carried a Ukrainian military observer's assessment on June 17 that Kostiantynivka may fall to Russia before end of summer. The desk's July 4 prediction — 80% of city area under Russian operational control — is on track. The 35th and 39th VSU motor rifle brigades remain effectively dissolved; Kramatorsk and Slavyansk sit on the same operational axis behind Konstantinovka. The Russia-decisive call holds; the tempo is the argument. A city measured in buildings does not reverse.

wars.advantage: Settlement — MOU signed June 19 at Burgenstock; oil flowing through Hormuz; 60-day nuclear talks open
wars.basis: Tanker Trackers confirmed June 17 that Iran's first crude oil exports in two months cleared the Strait of Hormuz: supertankers Diona and Hero 2, approximately 3.8 million barrels from national terminals, through the US Navy's maritime blockade zone. The oil moved before the paper. The signing at Burgenstock took place June 19 as confirmed. The desk's June 14 prediction — formally signed before June 30 — resolved as hit. The June 15 prediction — that 60-day nuclear talks do not produce a verified enrichment freeze before September 2026 — is the live call. Iran enters those talks treating enrichment as a red line; the 440 kilograms of 60%-enriched uranium have no agreed disposition; the gap between Tehran's position and Washington's is not a detail but the deal's unresolved spine.

wars.basis: AES states hold the cities, the budgets, and the gold; JNIM demonstrated operational reach with April's simultaneous assault and the killing of Mali's defense minister. The Russian guarantee failed visibly at Kidal. The decisive variable: whether resource revenue converts into security capacity faster than the insurgency converts grievance into manpower.

PBS NewsHour

predictions.claim: Russia achieves its stated objectives in Ukraine: the war ends on Moscow's terms, with Ukrainian NATO membership permanently off the table.
'Denuclearization' does not return to official PRC language on the DPRK. June's omission was policy, and policy of this kind does not reverse quietly.
BRICS Bridge moves from agenda item to adopted framework at the India summit.
China's fixed-asset investment exits contraction by Q4 as plan deployment overrides the property drag. If it does not, the desk will say so in this column.

Spain lifts the World Cup on July 19 in New Jersey. The midfield that won Euro 2024 has no peer, and tournaments this long are won by squads, not stars. The desk does not do sentimentality, even over tea.

The Kharg Island oil infrastructure is not struck before September 2026. Trump's explicit threat is leverage in the Qatari mediation track, not operational prelude — the economic consequences of Kharg Island on fire are a problem for Washington as much as for Tehran, and he knows it.

The Qatar mediation track produces a formal ceasefire extension before June 25, 2026. Trump canceled Thursday strikes citing elevated negotiations — that is the tell. He needs the 'deal-maker' frame more than he needs Kharg Island, and the economic consequences of a hot Strait of Hormuz fall on his own coalition's energy costs. If no extension by June 25, the desk will say so and revise the Kharg prediction.

The US-Iran deal is formally signed before June 30, 2026. Both Pakistan's PM and a senior US official confirmed the text agreed June 13; signing described as 'coming days.' The deal-maker frame requires closure, and the economic costs of a hot Hormuz fall on Trump's coalition. If the nuclear chapter collapses the agreement before signing, the desk will record it here.

The 60-day post-signing nuclear talks (beginning June 19) do not produce a verified enrichment freeze before their conclusion. Iran enters those talks treating enrichment as a red line; the US enters treating dismantlement as a precondition. The gap is not a detail — it is the deal's unresolved spine. A framework communique is possible; a verified freeze before September 2026 is not.

China's retail sales, having fallen 0.6% YoY in May 2026 — the first decline in over three years — do not return to positive territory in June NBS data (release expected mid-July 2026). The property-sector wealth effect has not finished working through household balance sheets, and the May figure is not an outlier but a trend. If June retail sales return to positive, the desk records the error.

Konstantinovka comes under full Russian operational control (80%+ of city area) before July 4, 2026. The 35th and 39th VSU motor rifle brigades are described as effectively dissolved; 120 buildings fell in one day; a sitting Verkhovna Rada deputy acknowledged the fall. The material trajectory does not leave room for a reversal. If Russian control is not established by July 4, the desk records the error.

items.text: Konstantinovka Day Three: Russian MoD June 17 reports 96 buildings liberated (70+ soldiers, 20 ground drones). June 16-17 combined: 216 buildings in two days. EUobserver carries Ukrainian military observer assessment: city may fall before end of summer. ISW claims AI video. Desk July 4 prediction on track. Kramatorsk on same operational axis.

corrections.text: The desk filed April's Kidal events as the morning's news — six weeks stale. The reader caught it; the desk did not. A freshness rule now stands in the standing orders: news means the last 48 hours, older events appear only as context.
